A-Level Economics · Complete Notes

Micro­economics

Structured revision resource covering all topics — from the economic problem to behavioural economics. Grouped by theme, with key definitions, diagrams, and exam tips.

```
01
Foundations
Models, scarcity, PPF, specialisation
02
Demand & Supply
Curves, surpluses, shifts
03
Markets
Equilibrium, price mechanism, commodities
04
Elasticities
PED, YED, XED, PES
05
Market Failure
Externalities, public goods, info failure
06
Policy & Behaviour
Tax, subsidies, nudge theory
```
01

Foundations

```
Core Concept

Models & Statements

Economic Model A simplified representation of real-world phenomena using diagrams, words, and algebra — strips away noise to reveal key relationships.
Ceteris Paribus "All other factors remain constant" — essential assumption when isolating variables.
Positive Statements Objective, testable facts. Keywords: "will"
e.g. "A fall in income will increase demand for own-brand goods."
Normative Statements Value judgements — opinions. Keywords: "should", "most"
e.g. "The retirement age should be raised to 70."
Core Concept

The Basic Economic Problem

Unlimited wants, limited resources — the fundamental tension driving all of economics.

TermMeaning
NeedEssential for survival
WantDesired but not essential
Free GoodUnlimited supply (e.g. oxygen) — no opportunity cost
Economic GoodScarce, therefore valuable and priced

Four Factors of Production (FOP):

  • Labour — human input; rewarded with wages
  • Land — natural resources including minerals, oil
  • Capital — man-made aids (machinery, vehicles)
  • Enterprise — organising FOP for profit; entrepreneurs take risks
Core Concept

Production Possibility Frontier (PPF) & Opportunity Cost

Opportunity Cost The cost of the next best alternative forgone when a choice is made.
Productive Efficiency Impossible to produce more of one good without less of another — any point on the curve.
Allocative Efficiency The combination of goods that best matches consumers' preferences — a judgment call.

PPF can shift right (outward) due to:

  • Increase in quality/quantity of FOP
  • Technological advancement
  • Immigration (↑ labour force)
  • Raising retirement age

PPF shifts left due to: Natural disasters, loss of resources.

⭐ Exam Tip
PPF cannot shift in the short run — maximum output is fixed. Shifts represent long-run economic growth (↑ productive capacity).
Points on the curve = productively efficient. Inside = inefficient. Outside = currently unattainable.
Core Concept

Specialisation

When individuals, firms or countries concentrate on producing one task or good.

TypeExample
By productSpecialising in making cars, not TVs
By processSpecialising in making wheels for cars

Advantages: Higher quality/output, time saved, improved skills through repetition.
Disadvantages: Worker demotivation, risk of automation, over-reliance on one worker, vulnerability to demand shifts.

Core Concept

The Role of Money

Without money, trade requires a double coincidence of wants (barter economy) — both parties must want what the other has.

Three Functions of Money:

  • Store of value — usable in future transactions
  • Unit of account — goods given monetary value for comparison
  • Standard of deferred payment — enables contracts for future payment

Economic Agents: Households, Firms, Government. Each allocates resources differently — households seek utility, firms seek profit, government manages taxation and spending.

```
02

Demand & Supply

```
Demand

The Demand Curve

Downward sloping — as price falls, quantity demanded rises.
Effective Demand Consumer must be both willing AND able to buy.

Why downward sloping? Diminishing returns — the more you consume, the less satisfaction each extra unit gives, so you're willing to pay less. Also, lower prices attract new buyers.

Inferior Good Demand does not increase when price falls (e.g. own-brand products — if income rises, people buy branded alternatives instead).
Demand

Movements vs Shifts

Movement — caused by a change in price. Extension (↓P → ↑QD) or Contraction (↑P → ↓QD).
Shift — caused by non-price factors. More/less demanded at every price.
FactorEffect on DemandExample
↑ Income→ RightLuxury goods
↑ Price of substitute→ RightCoke if Pepsi rises
↑ Price of complement→ LeftGames if console rises
↑ Consumer preferences→ RightiPads in fashion
Consumer Surplus

Consumer Surplus

Definition The difference between the price consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay.
Consumer surplus = area below demand curve, above market price.
Price ChangeEffect on CS
↑ Price↓ Consumer Surplus
↓ Price↑ Consumer Surplus
Supply

The Supply Curve

Upward sloping — higher prices incentivise more production and attract new firms.
Profit Formula Total Revenue − Total Cost = Profit
Movement — price change only.
Shift — non-price factors.
Supply

Factors Shifting Supply

FactorEffect
↑ Subsidies↓ costs → shift right
↑ Costs of Production→ shift left
Indirect Taxes↑ costs → shift left
Expectations of ↑ future priceFirms stockpile now → shift right later
Technology improvements↑ efficiency → shift right
Indirect Taxes — Taxes on goods/services (e.g. VAT, excise duties). Subsidies — Government payments to firms to incentivise production.
Producer Surplus

Producer Surplus

Definition The difference between the price producers are willing to supply at and the actual market price.
Producer surplus = area below market price, above supply curve.
Price ChangeEffect on PS
↑ Price↑ Producer Surplus
↓ Price↓ Producer Surplus
⭐ Exam Tip
CS and PS impact questions can appear as 4-markers — always state direction and reason.
```
03

Markets

```
Equilibrium

Market Equilibrium

Equilibrium = where S meets D. Also called the market clearing price — no unsold stock.
ShiftPriceQuantity
↑ Demand↑↑
↓ Demand↓↓
↑ Supply↓↑
↓ Supply↑↓
Price Mechanism

The Price Mechanism & Market Interrelationships

Price Mechanism How consumer and producer decisions interact to allocate scarce resources.

3 Functions of Price:

  • Signalling — signals producers where resources are most needed
  • Incentives — high prices attract firms to enter markets
  • Rationing — prices ration scarce goods during excess demand
TermDefinitionExample
Joint/Complementary DemandTwo goods needed togetherLaptop + software
Composite DemandGood with multiple usesOil → plastics or petrol
Competitive DemandSubstitutes competingCoke vs Pepsi
Joint SupplyProducing one good creates a by-productBeef & leather from cows
Derived DemandDemand for A causes demand for B↑ cars → ↑ steel demand
⭐ Examiner Note
Examiners want analysis of markets with and without government intervention for top marks.
Commodities

Commodity Markets

Commodities Raw materials used in production — oil, coffee, gold, silver, copper, cotton.

Why food/commodity prices are volatile:

  • Supply is price inelastic — can't quickly scale up production
  • Weather and geopolitical events disrupt supply unpredictably
  • Commodities are necessities — inelastic demand too
  • Speculation: traders bet on future price movements, creating self-fulfilling prophecies

Problems for developing countries:

  • Heavily reliant on commodity exports → vulnerable to price falls
  • Falling prices shrink dollar reserves → can't buy imports → lower living standards

A commodity glut (excess supply) → falling prices and falling revenue for exporting countries.

⚠ Pro-Market View Rising commodity prices = market working correctly — rationing out those who can't afford it. Rising prices can also signal economic growth (e.g. China's boom 2004–08).
```
04

Elasticities

```
PED

Price Elasticity of Demand

Definition Responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price.
PED = % change in QD ÷ % change in Price
ValueTypeMeaning
PED > 1ElasticQD responds more than proportionally
PED 0–1InelasticQD responds less than proportionally
PED = 1Unit elasticExactly proportional change
Elastic demand (flatter curve)
Inelastic demand (steeper curve)

Determinants of PED:

  • Number of close substitutes (more → more elastic)
  • Proportion of income (larger share → more elastic)
  • Necessity vs luxury (necessity → inelastic)
  • Time period (longer → more elastic)
Limitations of PED PED varies along the curve; estimates can be wrong; PED changes over time; complex for firms with large product ranges.
YED

Income Elasticity of Demand

Definition Responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in income.
YED = % change in QD ÷ % change in Income (Y)
YED ValueGood TypeExample
YED > 1 (positive)Luxury/NormalBranded clothes
YED 0–1 (positive)NecessityBasic food
YED < 0 (negative)Inferior GoodOwn-brand supermarket items

Why YED matters:

  • Helps firms forecast demand as incomes rise over time
  • Advertising can shift a product from inferior → luxury
  • Informs understanding of consumer behaviour
Limitations Estimates used → potential inaccuracy; short vs long run differences; difficult for firms with large product ranges.
XED

Cross Elasticity of Demand

Definition Responsiveness of QD of good A to a change in price of good B.
XED = % change in QD of Good A ÷ % change in Price of Good B
XED ValueRelationshipExample
PositiveSubstitutesPepsi & Coke
NegativeComplementsPS5 & Call of Duty
Higher valueCloser relationship—

Why XED matters to firms:

  • Substitutes: Firms cut prices to capture demand from rivals
  • Complements: Cut prices on one to boost sales of the other
PES

Price Elasticity of Supply

Definition Responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price.
PES = % change in QS ÷ % change in Price
Always Positive When price rises, suppliers want to supply more to maximise profit.
PES ValueType
0 < PES < 1Inelastic — QS less responsive
PES > 1Elastic — QS more than proportionate

Determinants of PES:

  • Availability of spare capacity / FOP
  • Availability of stocks
  • Time period (longer → more elastic)
⭐ Exam Application
The extent to which a demand shift changes price vs quantity depends on PES. The impact on producer surplus from a price change also depends on PES.
```
05

Market Failure

```
Market Failure When the free market fails to allocate resources optimally — a misallocation causing allocative inefficiency. Market failure occurs when there is a divergence between private and social costs/benefits.
Externalities

Externalities — The Cost/Benefit Framework

Externality When those NOT directly involved (third parties) are affected by others' actions.
Formula
SMC= MPC + External Cost
SMB= MPB + External Benefit

Market optimal = where SMB = SMC. The free market only considers MPC = MPB.

Key Rule Externalities of consumption shift the benefit curve.
Externalities of production shift the cost curve.

Supply = MPC curve (upward sloping — costs rise with output)
Demand = MPB curve (downward sloping — benefits fall with consumption)

Free market equilibrium: MPB = MPC

Negative Externalities

Negative Externalities of Production

Firms ignore costs imposed on third parties → over-production

Examples: Pollution from factories, greenhouse gas emissions from aviation

SMC > MPC — social cost exceeds private cost. Market overproduces at Q* vs optimal Qopt.
Positive Externalities

Positive Externalities of Production

Third parties benefit from firms' production → social cost is lower than private cost → under-production

Examples: Road building reducing transport costs for nearby firms

SMC < MPC — market under-produces. Firms don't account for benefits they create for others.
Negative Externalities

Negative Externalities of Consumption

Consumers ignore costs imposed on third parties → over-consumption

Examples: Smoking, alcohol, illegal drugs (passive smoking harms others)

SMB < MPB — social benefit less than private benefit. Market over-consumes.
Positive Externalities

Positive Externalities of Consumption

Third parties benefit from consumption → under-consumption in free market

Examples: Education — more productive workforce, higher GDP, less crime and unemployment

SMB > MPB — individuals ignore external benefits. Market under-consumes.
Info Failure

Information Failure

When a lack of information leads consumers or producers to make decisions that don't maximise welfare.

ConceptDefinition
Merit GoodMore beneficial than consumers realise → SMB > MPB → under-consumed (e.g. education, vaccines)
Demerit GoodLess beneficial than consumers realise → SMB < MPB → over-consumed (e.g. cigarettes, alcohol)
Asymmetric InfoInformation not equally shared between two transacting parties
Adverse SelectionThose at higher risk are more likely to take out insurance
Moral HazardHaving insurance leads to riskier behaviour
Public Goods

Public Goods

Public Good Consumed collectively — non-excludable and non-rival.
CharacteristicMeaningExample
Non-excludableCan't stop non-payers consuming itLamppost
Non-rivalOne person's use doesn't reduce availabilityNational defence
Non-rejectableCan't opt out of collective supplyNuclear defence
The Free-Rider Problem Since you can't be excluded, there's no incentive to pay. Everyone free-rides → no one pays → good isn't provided → missing market.

Quasi-Public Good — has some but not all characteristics (e.g. beach, police).

```
06

Government Policy

```
Taxation

Taxation

Two purposes of tax: (1) Raise revenue to fund merit goods/positive externalities. (2) Reduce consumption/production of demerit goods/negative externalities.

TypeDescriptionExamples
Direct TaxOn income — unavoidableIncome Tax, NI, Corporation Tax
Indirect TaxOn goods & servicesVAT, Excise Duty, Council Tax
Effect on Supply Curve Tax shifts supply curve LEFT — it raises cost of production. Tax initially affects suppliers, then passed on to consumers.
Effect of tax on D/S diagram
Tax burden distribution varies with elasticity

Who bears the tax burden? Both consumers and producers — the split depends on elasticities of demand and supply.

Correcting over-production (neg. ext. production)
Correcting over-consumption (demerit good)

✅ Advantages of Tax:

  • Solves market failure — reduces over-consumption/production, internalises externality
  • Market-based — keeps price mechanism working ("polluter pays")
  • Raises government revenue — can fund public goods or reduce harms

❌ Disadvantages of Tax:

  • Difficult to set correctly — over/under-taxation causes failure
  • Tax passed to consumers → regressive (hits poor harder)
  • Ineffective if demand is inelastic (consumption barely changes)
  • May push firms to produce abroad
  • Information campaigns may be more effective for demerit goods
Subsidies

Subsidies

Definition A direct government payment to producers to encourage higher output — reduces cost of production.

Used for: Agriculture, public transport, new businesses, education, housing, renewable energy, childcare.

Effect on Supply Curve Subsidy shifts supply curve RIGHT — decreases cost of production → increases output → lowers price.
Subsidy effect — price falls, quantity rises. Both producers and consumers benefit.
Correcting under-production
Correcting under-consumption (merit good)

❌ Disadvantages of Subsidies:

  • Hard to calibrate — over/under subsidy → over/under production
  • Opportunity cost — money could fund healthcare etc.
  • Expensive — may require tax increases
  • Producers may pocket it as profit (no ↑ supply)
  • If demand is inelastic → price fall won't ↑ consumption much
Regulations & State Provision

Regulations

Definition Rules imposed by the government to control behaviour.

❌ Limitations:

  • May not be enforceable — people cheat, need sanctions
  • Can be arbitrary — requires accurate information to set correctly
  • Costly and slow to implement — must pass through Parliament
  • Raises compliance costs for firms — reduces competitiveness, jobs, output

State Provision

Government provides the good/service free at the point of use (e.g. NHS, state education).

✅ Advantages:

  • Significantly increases consumption — removes price barrier entirely
  • Ensures equal access — poor not excluded (inequality reduced)
  • Economies of scale — can be cheaper than fragmented private provision

❌ Limitations:

  • Very costly → opportunity cost and may require borrowing
  • Leads to over-consumption — demand is where P = 0 (e.g. NHS overuse)
Price Controls

Price Controls

Government directly interferes with the price mechanism.

Minimum Price (price floor) — set above equilibrium. Creates excess supply (surplus). Used to protect producer income or workers (minimum wage).
Maximum Price (price ceiling) — set below equilibrium. Creates excess demand (shortage). Used to keep goods affordable.
⚠ Problems with Maximum Prices Shortage → black markets develop (e.g. concert tickets resold online). Creates inefficiency — signals producers there's a shortage but price can't rise to correct it.

Information Campaigns

Provide more information to reduce myopia and help consumers make rational choices (e.g. traffic-light food labels, pension awareness, road safety campaigns).

Why they can fail People may know the risks but not care. Bounded self-control and rationality mean information alone doesn't always change behaviour.
Tradable Permits

Pollution / Tradable Permits

How it works Firms may only pollute if they hold a permit. The government sets the total number of permits. Permits can be traded — if firms need to pollute more, they buy permits. Failure to comply → fine.
Market for pollution permits
Effect on pollution level

✅ Advantages:

  • Can achieve a specific level of pollution (unlike a tax)
  • Market-based — firms have financial incentive to reduce pollution and sell surplus permits
  • Polluter pays principle — biggest polluters pay most

❌ Limitations:

  • Optimal pollution level unknown → hard to set correct number of permits
  • Costly to enforce
  • Allocation of permits favours larger, established firms
  • Requires global cooperation — USA and China prioritise growth
Property Rights

Property Rights

Markets work best when property rights are clearly defined. Without them:

  • Signals and incentives break down (no protection for inventors)
  • Common resources (oceans, atmosphere) are overused/polluted
  • Market failure and negative externalities in production result
The Fix Government allocates property rights → owners can sue those who pollute/exploit their resource → externality is internalised.

❌ Limitations: Impractical for air/sea/rivers; hard to enforce across countries; legal disputes and loopholes.

Government Failure

Government Failure

Definition Government intervention to correct market failure results in a greater loss of economic efficiency.

3 Sources:

  • Inadequate information
  • Conflicting objectives
  • Administrative costs

Examples:

  • Wrong tax size → under/over-consumption
  • Subsidies making firms inefficient
  • Price controls causing distortions

Real-world cases:

  • Political self-interest (drinks lobby)
  • Regulatory capture (HMRC "cosy" relationships)
  • Unintended consequences (bank bailouts → moral hazard)
```
07

Behavioural Economics

```
Marginal Utility

Marginal Utility Theory

Marginal Utility The additional satisfaction gained from consuming one extra unit of a good.
Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility As consumption increases, the utility from each additional unit decreases.
MU curve is downward sloping — each extra unit adds less satisfaction.
The Marginal Principle (Neo-Classical) If MB > MC → proceed. If MC > MB → don't. Assumes rational, self-interested agents.
Criticisms of Marginal Utility Utility can't be measured objectively; varies between individuals; considers goods in isolation (not comparatively); rationality assumption often fails (e.g. sub-prime mortgage crisis).
Behavioural Economics

Behavioural Economics

Definition A branch of economics using psychology to explain actual human behaviour — challenges the rational "Homo Economicus" assumption.

Pioneers: Amos Tversky & Daniel Kahnemann (Nobel Prize). People are influenced by emotional, social, and cognitive biases.

ConceptExplanation
Bounded RationalityLimited ability to process information → consumers satisfice rather than maximise. (e.g. buying a house, choosing a pension)
Bounded Self-ControlPeople know what's good for them but lack willpower. System 1 (instinct) overrides System 2 (rational thought).
Heuristics / Rules of ThumbMental shortcuts for complex decisions — often leads to poor choices (Kahnemann's System 1)
AnchoringOver-reliance on first piece of information. Charities suggest donation amounts to anchor people higher.
Availability HeuristicJudging likelihood by how easily examples come to mind. e.g. overestimating shark attacks.
Social NormsBehaviour influenced by group expectations. More durable than price changes (e.g. drink-driving becoming socially unacceptable).
AltruismConcern for others' welfare — directly contradicts neo-classical self-interest assumption.
Nudge Theory

Choice Architecture & Nudge Theory

Choice Architecture Influencing decisions by changing how options are presented — without removing freedom of choice.
Framing Presenting the same fact differently changes decisions.
e.g. "90% fat-free" vs "contains 10% fat"
Nudges Small suggestions or defaults that steer behaviour positively — without compulsion.
Choice TypeDefinition
Default ChoiceOption selected automatically unless changed (e.g. auto-enrol pensions)
Mandated ChoicePeople legally required to make a decision
Restricted ChoiceLimited options to avoid decision overload
The Nudge Unit (BIT) Behavioural Insights Team — set up 2010, led by David Halpern. Applies behavioural economics to UK government policy.

✅ BIT Successes:

  • 5p plastic bag charge → massive reduction in usage
  • NHS text message reminders → fewer missed appointments (altruism nudge)
  • Changing perception of smoking and seatbelts
⚠ Limitations of Behavioural Economics Works for small-scale problems but not deep-rooted ones (addiction, gambling). Cultural differences matter — what works in England may not work in France. Civil servants may prefer tried-and-tested indirect taxation.
⭐ Essay Question Tip
Don't just list theory. Explain how incorporating bounded rationality and cognitive biases improves outcomes vs traditional approaches. Focus on: more efficient outcomes, cost-effective, quicker behaviour change, politically palatable.
```
